Q1: Honestly, my study routine was pretty scattered. I'd tell myself I'd study at certain times, but then I'd get distracted by other stuff—hanging out with friends, my extracurriculars, whatever. I'd usually end up cramming things in whenever I remembered or when something was due soon. I know I put in around 23 hours a week, but it felt like it was all over the place rather than organized.
Q2: My pattern definitely varied a lot based on deadlines. If something big was coming up, I'd suddenly be studying way more, but during quieter weeks I'd barely touch anything. I'm not great at keeping a consistent schedule—I just kind of go with the flow and deal with things as they come up. Regular times each week? That's not really my style.
Q3: Usually what got me to log in was either a notification about something due or realizing I'd missed a few days and probably should check what's going on. Sometimes my classmates would mention something in the discussion forums and I'd feel like I should at least see what people were talking about. It wasn't really a habit, more like a reaction to external pressure.
Q4: There were brief moments where I'd log in more regularly, like when we had group discussions going or when I was actually interested in a topic. But it never really stuck as a routine. I'd get into it for a week or two, then drift off again when something else caught my attention or I just felt tired of it all.
Q5: The video lectures were probably the most useful for me since I learn better by listening. I could put them on while doing other things or just zone in when I needed to understand something. The discussion posts were okay too because hearing other people's takes helped me process the material better than just reading on my own.
Q6: I definitely ignored a lot of the reading materials and some of the optional practice exercises. Reading walls of text just doesn't work for my brain—I zone out after a few paragraphs. If it wasn't going to be on an exam or required for an assignment, I usually skipped it. I figured I could get by without doing everything.
Q7: An activity felt worth my time if I could see how it connected to something I'd actually be graded on, or if it was genuinely interesting. If it felt like busywork or just checking boxes, I'd lose interest fast. I also needed it to not take forever—if something seemed like it would eat up hours for minimal payoff, I'd probably skip it.
Q8: Yeah, optional stuff felt way different. Required activities had this weight to them because I knew they'd affect my grade, so I'd push through even when I didn't want to. Optional things? I'd look at them, think 'that might be helpful,' and then never actually do them. There's just no urgency without consequences.
Q9: Definitely. I'd download lecture recordings and listen to them on my phone when I was commuting or working out. I also talked through concepts with friends from class pretty often, which helped me understand things way better than staring at a screen. A lot of my actual learning happened away from the LMS.
Q10: There were times I'd be going over notes with a friend for hours, just talking through stuff and quizzing each other, and none of that would show up anywhere. Or I'd re-listen to downloaded audio while doing chores. The LMS only sees when I click things, not when I'm actually thinking about the material.
Q11: Yeah, all the time. I'd download a video or save some slides, watch or read through them once online, and then keep coming back to them offline. Especially the audio stuff—I'd listen to lectures multiple times but only that first download would register. It was more convenient than logging in every time.
Q12: There were definitely rough patches, especially mid-semester when everything piled up at once. I had extracurriculars demanding my time, assignments from multiple courses due around the same dates, and I just felt stretched thin. My stress was through the roof even though I tried to stay motivated. Those weeks I barely engaged with anything properly.
Q13: Absolutely. When I was exhausted or overwhelmed, I'd log into the LMS but just kind of stare at it without really doing anything productive. Fatigue made it hard to focus on videos or readings. And when I had competing responsibilities—like stuff for my clubs or family things—the course always seemed to get pushed to the back burner.
Q14: More times than I'd like to admit. I'd open up materials, maybe have a video playing, but my mind would be somewhere else entirely. I'd technically be 'studying' but not actually absorbing anything. It was like going through the motions without being present. Those sessions felt pretty pointless afterward.
Q15: I put off a lot of assignments even when I knew they mattered. Part of it was just not feeling like doing them in the moment, and part was this weird anxiety about starting something I wasn't sure I could do well. I'd tell myself I'd get to it later, and then 'later' became 'last minute panic mode.'
Q16: Deadlines basically controlled everything. I'd be pretty inactive until a deadline was approaching, then suddenly I'd be all over the LMS trying to catch up. It wasn't healthy, but that pressure was the main thing that actually got me to engage. Without deadlines, I probably would have drifted even more.
Q17: Feedback had some effect, but honestly I didn't always check it right away. When I did get good feedback, it motivated me a bit for the next assignment. But if the feedback was critical or I got a lower grade than expected, I'd kind of avoid thinking about it. It's easier to just move on than dwell on what went wrong.
Q18: The course structure was okay, but having nine online courses meant everything kind of blurred together. When modules were clearly laid out with obvious next steps, I'd engage more. But when things felt vague or I had to figure out what to do next on my own, I'd often just not bother until it became urgent.
Q19: Having video content made things easier for me since I'm an auditory learner. But the amount of reading and written assignments made engagement harder. Also, when discussion posts felt forced or artificial, I didn't really want to participate. The mindfulness intervention they had us do was interesting but didn't really change my study habits much.
Q20: I think that happens when people are trying to figure things out but struggling to actually produce work. Like, they're clicking around, watching videos, maybe re-reading stuff, but they can't translate that into completed assignments. It could be confusion, anxiety about submitting imperfect work, or just not knowing where to start.
Q21: That actually sounds kind of like me in some ways. I didn't log in constantly, but I found ways to study that worked for me outside the system. If someone's efficient with their time or learns well through other methods, they don't need to be clicking around the LMS all day to do okay on exams.
Q22: Course logs can show patterns—like when you're active, what you click on, how long you spend on things. But they miss the quality of that engagement and all the learning that happens offline. They can't tell if you're actually understanding material or just going through motions. They see activity, not learning.
Q23: Being engaged means actually caring about what you're learning and putting in genuine effort to understand it. It's not just showing up or clicking through materials—it's being mentally present and wanting to get something out of the experience. For me, real engagement is when I'm curious and thinking critically, not just surviving until the deadline.
Q24: The LMS wouldn't show how much I learned from conversations with classmates, or how I processed lectures while doing other activities. It wouldn't capture my high stress levels or the anxiety that sometimes kept me from engaging even when I wanted to. And it definitely wouldn't show that despite my scattered approach, I was resilient enough to push through and pass.
